name: Tellurium
name_origin: From the Latin 'tellus' meaning 'earth', named by Martin Heinrich Klaproth in 1798 to complement selenium (named for the moon).

discovery.discoverer: Franz-Joseph Muller von Reichenstein (isolated); named by Martin Heinrich Klaproth in 1798

occurrence.abundance_summary: Tellurium is one of the rarest stable elements in Earth's crust (about 0.001 ppm), comparable to platinum. It occurs as native tellurium and in tellurides such as calaverite (AuTe2) and sylvanite. It is recovered chiefly as a byproduct of copper electrolytic refining.

compounds.name: Tellurium dioxide
compounds.summary: A white solid used in optical glass for acousto-optic devices and as a key component in tellurite glasses.

compounds.name: Hydrogen telluride
compounds.summary: A toxic, foul-smelling colorless gas, the heaviest stable hydride of group 16.

compounds.name: Cadmium telluride
compounds.summary: A II-VI semiconductor widely used in thin-film photovoltaic solar panels and infrared optics.

compounds.name: Bismuth telluride
compounds.summary: A premier thermoelectric material used in Peltier coolers and waste-heat power generation.

compounds.name: Tellurium hexafluoride
compounds.summary: A colorless, highly toxic gas analogous to SF6, used in research as a chemical etchant.

uses: Cadmium telluride (CdTe) thin-film solar photovoltaic panels
Thermoelectric devices based on bismuth telluride for cooling and power generation
Alloying additive to steel and copper to improve machinability
Phase-change materials (Ge-Sb-Te chalcogenides) for rewritable optical discs and memory
Vulcanizing agent and catalyst in the rubber industry
reactivity_summary: Tellurium is a brittle, silvery-white metalloid with a metallic luster. In its crystalline form it is stable in air; finely divided tellurium burns in air to form TeO2. It is insoluble in water but dissolves in nitric acid, aqua regia, and concentrated sulfuric acid. Tellurium reacts with halogens to form tetrahalides and with most metals to form tellurides. Even small exposures can cause humans to exude a strong, garlic-like odor on the breath.
isotopes_summary: Tellurium has eight naturally occurring isotopes, of which six are stable or considered stable, and two (tellurium-128 and tellurium-130) are radioactive but with extraordinarily long half-lives. Tellurium-128 has the longest measured half-life of any nuclide (about 2.2 x 10^24 years) via double beta decay. Tellurium-130 is the most abundant isotope (34.08%).

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Tellurium
CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 97th Edition (2016-2017)
IUPAC Atomic Weights of the Elements 2021
